bugs.description: The DFS in search() explores an exponential number of candidate b- without any memoization or pruning, leading to a time limit exceed for the worst‑case n=10 cases (especially when no solution exists).

The DFS in search() explores an exponential number of candidate b- without any memoization or pruning, leading to a time limit exceed for the worst‑case n=10 cases (especially when no solution exists).

The search only generates new b values as x±ai for existing b and original a_i. This does not guarantee that all possible b sets are reachable, so a valid solution may be missed and the program can output NO incorrectly.

The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidates (up to ~200^depth) without memoization or pruning, which can cause time limit exceeded for worst‑case inputs (n=10 with many distinct a_i).

The search only generates new b values as x±ai for existing b and original a_i. This does not guarantee that all possible b sets are reachable, so a valid solution may be missed and the program can output NO incorrectly.

The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidates (up to ~200^depth) without memoization or pruning, which can cause time limit exceeded for worst‑case inputs (n=10 with many distinct a_i).

The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b-sets (up to (2n)^{n-1} possibilities). For n=10 this leads to a huge search tree and causes the program to exceed time limits on many inputs.

The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b-sets (up to (2n)^{n-1} possibilities). For n=10 this leads to a huge search tree and causes the program to exceed time limits on many inputs.
The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b sets without memoization or pruning, leading to a time limit exceeded for worst‑case inputs (n=10, no solution).
The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b sets without memoization or pruning, leading to a time limit exceeded for worst‑case inputs (n=10, no solution).
Exponential DFS without memoization leads to huge search space; for worst-case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct non‑zero a_i) the algorithm explores astronomically many states and will time out.
The candidate generation includes values already present in the current set b, causing recursive calls with the same set (b|{c} == b) and unnecessary depth consumption.
No memoization or visited‑state pruning is used, so the same b set can be examined repeatedly with different remaining depth, further increasing runtime.
Exponential DFS without memoization leads to huge search space; for worst-case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct non‑zero a_i) the algorithm explores astronomically many states and will time out.
The candidate generation includes values already present in the current set b, causing recursive calls with the same set (b|{c} == b) and unnecessary depth consumption.
No memoization or visited‑state pruning is used, so the same b set can be examined repeatedly with different remaining depth, further increasing runtime.

The recursive search explores many duplicate states because it recurses on candidates that are already present in the current set b. This leads to an exponential blow‑up (up to ~3^n * n) and causes time limit exceeded for worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct non‑zero a_i where the answer is NO).

The recursive search explores many duplicate states because it recurses on candidates that are already present in the current set b. This leads to an exponential blow‑up (up to ~3^n * n) and causes time limit exceeded for worst‑case inputs (e.g., n=10 with distinct non‑zero a_i where the answer is NO).

The recursive search explores all possible additions of new b values without any pruning or memoization. In the worst case (e.g., n=10 with values that cannot be covered), the branching factor can be up to ~200 per level, leading to an exponential explosion (≈200^9) and causing a time limit exceeded. The algorithm is not efficient enough for the given constraints.

The recursive search explores all possible additions of new b values without any pruning or memoization. In the worst case (e.g., n=10 with values that cannot be covered), the branching factor can be up to ~200 per level, leading to an exponential explosion (≈200^9) and causing a time limit exceeded. The algorithm is not efficient enough for the given constraints.
